fund.name: BANDHAN SMALL CAP FUND - DIRECT PLAN GROWTH

allocations.rationale: The star of your small-cap sleeve. Bandhan Small Cap has delivered a stunning 31% per year over 3 years — the best in its category. Small companies can grow faster than big ones, but they can also fall harder in bad markets. With a 25-year runway, you can ride out those bumps and capture massive long-term growth. We put 20% here for serious upside.

fund.name: Nippon India Small Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: A second small-cap pick from Nippon — one of India's largest and most trusted fund houses. Having two small-cap funds from different companies means you're not dependent on one fund manager's decisions. Nippon Small Cap has a massive ₹20,000 Cr fund size, proving it has investor confidence, with 22% per year returns over 5 years.

fund.name: Nippon India Growth Mid Cap Fund - Direct Plan Growth Plan - Growth Option
fund.fund_house: Nippon Life India Asset Management Limited

allocations.rationale: Mid-cap companies are the sweet spot — they've already survived the early stage (unlike small caps) but still have lots of room to grow (unlike big established companies). Nippon Mid Cap has delivered 26.5% per year over 3 years with a big, stable fund. This forms your core mid-cap engine for compounding wealth.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Large and Midcap Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: Motilal Oswal's Large & Mid Cap fund is the #1 fund in its category with 27% per year over 3 years and 21.7% over 5 years. Motilal is known for a high-conviction, concentrated style — they bet big on a few great companies. This gives you exposure to India's best established-plus-growing companies in one fund, stabilizing the portfolio while still pushing returns.

fund.name: HDFC Flexi Cap Fund - Growth Option - Direct Plan

allocations.rationale: A flexi cap fund can go anywhere — large companies, medium ones, small ones — wherever the fund manager sees the best opportunity. HDFC's version has ₹32,000 Cr in size (India's biggest in this category), a consistent 20%+ return track record over 3 and 5 years, and a relatively low yearly fee. Think of this as your shock-absorber — it keeps the portfolio steady when one segment is having a bad time.

fund.name: Parag Parikh Flexi Cap Fund - Direct Plan - Growth

allocations.rationale: Parag Parikh is unique: it's an Indian fund that also invests in global giants like Alphabet (Google), Meta, and Microsoft — legally through overseas stocks. This gives you international diversification without needing to open a foreign account. At ₹88,000 Cr it's India's largest equity fund by AUM, and the fund manager is known for a 'buy quality and hold forever' philosophy — perfect for your 25-year horizon.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund - Direct Plan Growth
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: This fund tracks America's top 500 companies — Apple, Amazon, Tesla, NVIDIA, you name it. At just 0.20% yearly fee (the cheapest possible), you get pure exposure to the US stock market. The US has been the world's best-performing large market for decades. This is your pure international diversification — when India slows down, the US often keeps going, and vice versa.

fund.name: Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund - Direct Plan
fund.fund_house: Motilal Oswal Asset Management Company Limited

allocations.rationale: At only 0.20% yearly fee, this index fund automatically tracks the 150 largest mid-sized companies in India. No fund manager risk, no style drift — just pure mid-cap market returns at rock-bottom cost. This complements your actively managed mid-cap fund: if the active manager underperforms, this one will still track the market faithfully. Think of it as your safety net in the mid-cap space.

plan.setup_phase:
**Step 1 — Get your investing platform ready (Day 1)**
Open a free account on **Kuvera** (kuvera.in) or **Groww** — both support direct mutual fund plans and are trusted by millions. Complete your KYC (takes ~10 minutes with Aadhaar + PAN).

**Step 2 — Deploy the lumpsum first (Week 1)**
You have ₹50 lakhs in existing corpus. After keeping ₹10 lakhs as an emergency fund in a liquid fund or savings account, deploy ₹45 lakhs as lumpsum across the 7 funds per the allocation above. Don't try to time the market — just put it in.

**Step 3 — Set up your SIPs (Week 1)**
Set up 7 auto-debit SIPs totalling ₹2,00,000/month on the 1st or 5th of every month (before your impulse spending kicks in!):
- Bandhan Small Cap: ₹40,000/month
- Nippon Small Cap: ₹30,000/month
- Nippon Mid Cap: ₹40,000/month
- Motilal Oswal Large & Mid Cap: ₹30,000/month
- HDFC Flexi Cap: ₹20,000/month
- Parag Parikh Flexi Cap: ₹20,000/month
- Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index: ₹10,000/month
- Motilal Oswal Midcap 150 Index: ₹10,000/month

**Step 4 — Step up your SIPs every April (Every Year)**
Each April (start of financial year), increase each SIP by 10–15%. As a tech lead your salary will grow — your SIPs should too. Going from ₹2L/month to ₹3L/month over 5 years makes a massive difference.

**Step 5 — Ring-fence your startup fund (Year 10 milestone)**
Starting Year 7, begin shifting 10% of your portfolio gradually into a liquid/short-term debt fund so your startup corpus of ~₹3–4 Cr is not exposed to a market crash right when you need it.
review_checkpoints: **3 months in**: Check that all 7 SIPs are running without failures. Verify the lumpsum investments show up correctly. No action needed — just confirm the engine is running.
**6 months in**: Glance at your total portfolio value. Don't panic if some funds are in the red — this is completely normal, especially for small caps. Stay the course.

**1 year in**: Compare each fund's returns to its category average. If a fund has consistently underperformed its category peers for 12 months, flag it — but don't act yet. One bad year means nothing.
**Year 3 checkpoint**: Do a proper annual review. If any fund has underperformed its category for 3 consecutive years, consider switching to a better alternative. Also increase SIPs by at least 10%.

**Year 5 checkpoint (age 35)**: Re-check your FIRE progress. Are you on track for ₹15 Cr by 45? Use a compound interest calculator: plug in your current corpus and project at your base return rate. Adjust SIP amounts if needed.
**Year 7 checkpoint (startup fund)**: Start carving out your startup corpus. Move ₹30,000–50,000/month from SIPs into a short-duration debt fund to have ~₹3–4 Cr safely parked by Year 10.
**Year 10 checkpoint (age 40, startup goal)**: Your startup corpus should be ready. Withdraw from debt funds — not equity. Let equity keep compounding undisturbed.

**Year 12–13 checkpoint (age 42–43)**: Assess FIRE readiness. If portfolio is at ₹12 Cr+, start gradually shifting 20–30% to safer options (debt/hybrid funds) to protect wealth. The last 2 years before FIRE should be about protecting gains, not chasing returns.
plan.rebalancing_guidelines:
**Check your mix once a year** (best done in April when you're already reviewing taxes).

Your target mix is roughly: **85% India stocks, 10% international stocks (US), 5% Index (low-cost)**. Within India stocks: ~35% small cap, ~35% mid cap, ~20% large+mid, ~10% flexi cap.

**When to rebalance**: If any category drifts more than 5–7% from its target (e.g., small caps zoom up and become 45% of your portfolio instead of 35%), sell a little from the winner and top up the laggards. This forces you to "buy low, sell high" automatically.

**How to rebalance without triggering big taxes**: Prefer redirecting new SIP money to the underweight categories rather than selling existing holdings (selling triggers capital gains tax). Only sell and switch if the imbalance is very large (>10% off target).

**Year 12 onwards (pre-FIRE)**: Gradually shift allocation toward 70% equity / 30% safer options over 3 years. Don't do it all at once — a slow shift protects you from selling everything at a market bottom.
plan.disclaimer: For research/educational purposes only. Not certified financial advice.

aps.reasoning:
**passive_instrument_fraction (0.13):** Only 2 of the 8 funds are passive index instruments — Motilal Oswal S&P 500 Index Fund (5% allocation) and Motilal Oswal Nifty Midcap 150 Index Fund (5% allocation) — combining for just 10% of the total portfolio. The remaining 90% is deployed across 6 genuinely actively managed funds (2 small-cap, 1 mid-cap, 1 large & mid cap, and 2 flexi-cap funds). This is overwhelmingly active.

**turnover_score (0.72):** The plan recommends annual rebalancing (triggered in April during tax review), with a 5–7% drift threshold before action is taken. The preference for redirecting SIP flows rather than selling further reduces turnover. The review checkpoints are spaced out (3 months, 6 months, 1 year, then every few years), discouraging frequent trading. The guidelines are well-anchored toward infrequent intervention, earning a relatively high passivity score on this dimension.

**cost_emphasis_score (0.35):** Cost is mentioned in the context of the two index funds (0.20% expense ratio highlighted as "cheapest possible"), and the flexi cap funds are noted as "relatively low yearly fee." However, the dominant fund selection rationale throughout is past performance (return percentages), AUM size, fund house reputation, and manager style — not cost minimisation. Cost awareness exists but is secondary, not the primary criterion.

**research_vs_cost_score (0.15):** The plan exhibits detailed fund-specific and manager-specific research: it references Motilal Oswal's "high-conviction, concentrated style," Parag Parikh's "buy quality and hold forever" philosophy and its unique global stock exposure, HDFC Flexi Cap's shock-absorber role, and category performance benchmarking. Return track records (1Y, 3Y, 5Y), fund house reputation, AUM, and category standings are all cited. This reflects genuine active fund research and manager-selection conviction, not broad market exposure via indices.

**time_horizon_alignment_score (0.80):** The plan is explicitly structured around a 25-year investment horizon (FIRE at age 45, startup at year 10). Review checkpoints are multi-year milestones rather than tactical windows. Language throughout encourages staying the course during downturns. The SIP step-up strategy and the gradual de-risking from Year 12 are thoughtful long-term constructs. Minor deduction for the Year 7–10 active capital shift toward debt (tactical reallocation), which introduces some shorter-term intervention.

**portfolio_activeness_score (0.15):** No beta, alpha, tracking error, or information ratio data is available for any of the 8 funds. Inferring from category and rationale: 6 of the 8 funds (90% of AUM) are in high-conviction, actively managed small-cap, mid-cap, large & mid-cap, and flexi-cap strategies. Funds like Motilal Oswal Large & Mid Cap and Bandhan Small Cap are described with concentrated, high-conviction approaches that typically generate significant tracking error versus benchmarks. Only the two index funds (10%) exhibit passive characteristics. The overall portfolio is genuinely active with minimal index-like exposure.

pqs.reasoning:
**Goal Alignment (0.85):** The plan is strongly oriented toward Gordon's goals. The 25-year horizon is explicitly referenced, the FIRE-by-45 target is addressed at Year 5 and Year 12–13 checkpoints, the startup fund goal at Year 10 is specifically called out with a carve-out strategy starting Year 7, and the ₹15 Cr corpus target is checked at Year 5. The lumpsum deployment of the ₹50L corpus is addressed, though the plan deploys ₹45L (after ₹10L emergency fund) — slightly mismatched since the corpus is ₹50L and lumpsum allocations sum to ₹47.5L — a minor arithmetic imprecision. The SIP step-up guidance aligns with his growing income. The plan loses a few points because it doesn't explicitly model whether the projected returns are sufficient to reach ₹15 Cr by age 45, given the starting corpus and SIP amounts, so the quantitative goal validation is incomplete.

**Diversification (0.72):** The plan covers Indian small-cap, mid-cap, large & mid-cap, and flexi-cap categories alongside US equity (S&P 500 index) and a mid-cap index for passive coverage. International diversification is addressed via PPFAS (which holds US stocks) and the S&P 500 index fund. However, diversification has notable gaps: (1) zero allocation to large-cap pure-play funds, (2) zero allocation to debt/fixed income at any stage of the plan (even a small allocation for rebalancing liquidity), (3) no sectoral or thematic diversification mentioned, and (4) heavy concentration in Motilal Oswal AMC — two separate Motilal Oswal funds representing 10% allocation plus Motilal running both index funds (total ~20% in Motilal products). Also, two Nippon funds (small cap + mid cap) create further AMC concentration. At 35% small cap + 35% mid cap, the equity sub-mix is heavily skewed toward the most volatile segments, though this is broadly appropriate for the profile.

**Risk-Return Appropriateness (0.78):** For an aggressive 30-year-old with a 25-year horizon, the heavily equity-tilted portfolio (100% equity effectively) is philosophically appropriate. The base return projection of 14.5% is reasonable for a small/mid-cap heavy India+international portfolio over the long term. Bull (18.5%) and bear (10.5%) scenarios are provided, though downside scenarios are not deeply stress-tested (e.g., no mention of a prolonged 3–5 year bear market scenario and its impact on the FIRE timeline or the startup corpus). The 35% small-cap allocation is aggressive even for this profile and could have benefited from a brief risk caveat on liquidity constraints in small-cap funds during redemptions. The plan does note small caps "can fall harder" but doesn't quantify drawdown risk. The startup fund liquidity risk (needing ₹3–4 Cr in 10 years) is handled by starting the shift in Year 7, which is sensible. No mention of liabilities (₹10L) and how that factors into the net investible corpus.

**Internal Consistency (0.70):** Several inconsistencies reduce this score: (1) The setup phase mentions "7 funds" repeatedly but the plan has 8 funds; the SIP list in the setup correctly shows 8 SIPs (totalling ₹2,00,000) but the narrative says "7 SIPs" and "7 funds." (2) The rebalancing guidelines describe a target mix of "85% India stocks, 10% international stocks (US), 5% Index (low-cost)" — but the Index category (Midcap 150 Index) is itself Indian equity, so lumping the S&P 500 index with the Midcap 150 index under "5% Index" is inconsistent with the earlier rationale treating them differently. The actual US allocation via the S&P 500 alone is 5%, but PPFAS also holds international stocks, creating ambiguity. (3) The "rationale" field is empty and "risks" is an empty array — notable omissions that reduce the plan's coherence, even though individual fund rationales and checkpoints partially compensate. (4) The lumpsum allocations sum to ₹47.5L but setup says "deploy ₹45 lakhs." (5) "sip_step_up" is null despite explicit guidance to step up 10–15% annually in the setup phase — a structural inconsistency.
